---
title: "4.10 Implementing ArrayList Algorithms"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Implementing ArrayList Algorithms

### Standard array algorithm patterns carry over to dynamic lists

This topic maps familiar ideas onto `ArrayList`:

::: {.tight-list}
- sum, average, min, and max
- property tests
- duplicates and pairs
- shift, rotate, and reverse
- parallel data-structure traversal
:::

The main change is method-based access and size-changing behavior.

## Add and Remove During Algorithms

### Students need to trace list mutation precisely

The textbook retains direct tracing of:

::: {.tight-list}
- `add`
- `add(index, obj)`
- `remove`
- shifting side effects after insertion or deletion
:::

This is foundational because later algorithms depend on understanding how list contents move.

## Min, Max, Sum, and Average

### The same loop patterns still apply

Retained structure:

::: {.tight-list}
- use traversal to visit each list element
- maintain accumulators or current best values
- combine loops with `if` conditions as needed
- use wrapper values because the list stores objects
:::

Students should recognize that the pattern is familiar even though the collection type changed.

## Finding a Property

### List algorithms still rely on boolean checks inside loops

The AP-style patterns are the same as with arrays:

::: {.tight-list}
- determine whether at least one element has a property
- determine whether all elements have a property
- count how many elements have a property
:::

Only the access syntax changes from `array[i]` to `list.get(i)` or an enhanced `for` variable.

## Pairs, Duplicates, and Nested Loops

### Some list problems still need two-level iteration

The textbook keeps nested-loop reasoning for:

::: {.tight-list}
- consecutive pairs
- duplicate detection
- pair generation
:::

Students should explicitly justify when one loop is insufficient.

## Shift, Rotate, Reverse, and Parallel Structures

### Reordering and multi-list traversal combine several earlier ideas

Retained algorithm families:

::: {.tight-list}
- shift or rotate values in a list
- reverse list order
- traverse multiple arrays or `ArrayList` objects in sync
:::

This is where students need to balance index control, method use, and mutation effects.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- trace mutation caused by add/remove operations inside list algorithms
- write min/max/sum/average loops over `ArrayList`
- implement boolean-property and counting patterns
- use nested loops for duplicate and pair algorithms
- explain how parallel data structures are traversed together
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain how familiar array algorithms transfer to `ArrayList`
- distinguish when traversal can use enhanced `for` versus indexed loops
- explain the mutation effects of insertion and removal
- identify when nested loops are needed
- explain how two parallel lists can be traversed in step
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
